comments.text: As @Alfaiz wrote, Ákos Dúcz and I have posted our preprint "A unit-distance graph in the plane with independence ratio below 1/4" claiming to answer the second part of the question in the negative. We build on [MRVZ23], which is already cited in the current write-up. It is not entirely clear what resolving "Estimate $f(n)$", or in Erdős’s original phrasing, "Determine $f(n)$ as accurately as possible", should mean. If this is interpreted as determining the exact value of $\inf f(n)/n$ as @StijnC suggests, I believe none of us will ever resolve that. :) Dear Thomas (@TFBloom), do you have a protocol for questions with two parts like this, where only one part has been resolved? To be clear, I do not mean that even the second part should already be marked as resolved: our preprint has just appeared, and [MRVZ23] itself is still under peer review. (We spent one and a half years trying to find a more illuminating proof of [MRVZ23, Theorem 3], gave up, and submitted it in March 2025.)
This question has been answered in the negative by A. Dúcz and D. Varga in [DuVa26].

Depending how one reads the problem, it s the second question that is resolved. In this case, one could say that the estimate $f(n)=(m_1+o(1))n$ would resolve the problem if true (lower order terms would not be necessary).

Let me note that our not-yet-peer-reviewed paper [FCN4] makes progress on this question by presenting unit distance graphs with independence ratio arbitrarily close to 1/4. To the best of our knowledge, this is the first improvement of the trivial 2n/7 upper bound, to n/4+o(n). It is reasonable to conjecture that either Croft's 0.22936 or our 1/4 is sharp, [FCN4] Conjecture 1 states the latter. [FCN4] Máté Matolcsi, Imre Z. Ruzsa, Dániel Varga, Pál Zsámboki - The fractional chromatic number of the plane is at least 4, arXiv:2311.10069 (The site has been updated to address this comment.)

The problem above was solved by G.~Csizmadia in his paper \emph{On the independence number of minimum distance graphs}, Discrete Comput. Geom. \textbf{20}, 179--187 (1998). \url{https://doi.org/10.1007/PL00009381}. In this work, Csizmadia studies the unit-distance graph defined on a finite planar point set whose minimum pairwise distance is~1, and asks for the size of the largest independent set, that is, a subset containing no two points at distance exactly~1. Csizmadia improved an earlier result of Pollack by proving that every such set of \(n\) points contains an independent subset of size at least\[ \frac{9}{35}\,n \approx 0.2571\,n. \]In particular,\[ f(n) \ge \frac{9}{35}\,n > \frac{1}{4}\,n, \]so the conjectured lower bound \(f(n) \ge n/4\) is true (and even slightly improved). The paper also provides an \(O(n\log n)\) algorithm which constructs such a large independent subset for any given configuration of \(n\) points. \bigskip \noindent References [Cs98] G. Csizmadia, \emph{On the independence number of minimum distance graphs}, Discrete Comput. Geom. \textbf{20}, 179--187 (1998). \url{https://doi.org/10.1007/PL00009381}. [ACMVZ23] G. Ambrus, A. Csiszárik, M. Matolcsi, D. Varga, and P. Zsámboki, \emph{The density of planar sets avoiding unit distances}, arXiv:2207.14179 (2023). [Cr67] H. T. Croft, \emph{Incidence incidents}, Eureka (1967), 22--26. [LaRo72] D. G. Larman and C. A. Rogers, \emph{The realization of distances within sets in Euclidean space}, Mathematika (1972), 1--24.
Isn't that about problem 1066? There's an extra assumption on the minimum pairwise distance.

Wouter is correct. See https://www.renyi.hu/~p_erdos/1987-27.pdf for the paper and the two questions under 5 (second half page 171) This paper of Csizmadia is mentioned in https://www.erdosproblems.com/forum/thread/1066, where also a strengthening by Swanepoel is listed.
